real_insight: Engineers who have actually internalized a rule produce rule-fluent artifacts; engineers who claim to have transcended a rule but produce rule-violating artifacts have not transcended it — the artifact is the test, not the self-report.
objection: The two-sided framing (rule-hostage vs. premature-mastery) is good and not generic. The 'symmetry' framing slightly tilts toward elegance over precision — in real teams the premature-mastery pathology is far more common than the chronically rule-hostage one, and naming them as equally weighted is a small move toward editorial balance over engineering reality. Otherwise: the artifacts named are concrete (ESLint config, on-call playbook, Sentry, Datadog, design-patterns book, hexagonal architecture, GitHub Actions security policy, Kubernetes admission controllers, Postgres locking semantics), the failure mode is named precisely (hostile lookup-pattern decoupled from artifact quality), and the falsifiability clause (artifact, not self-report) is the kind of clause that distinguishes this from a fortune cookie. I would PASS this on a code review.
